Election.title: BV2168 — FairVote's Condorcet hypothetical, counted — the Moderate is the majority's head-to-head choice
Election.description: FairVote's article 'Why the Condorcet Criterion Is Less Important Than It Seems' (Alec Slatky, 2010) argues from a hypothetical: 'a strong liberal who commands between 40% to 50% of the vote, a moderate with about 10% to 15%, and a strong conservative between 40% and 50%.' This election counts exactly that electorate: 100 voters — Liberal 45 first choices, Moderate 12, Conservative 43, with each pole ranking the Moderate second. The Moderate is the Condorcet winner by real majorities (55-45 over Liberal, 57-43 over Conservative) — it is majorities, not the 12%, who choose the Moderate against either rival. STAR elects the Moderate (score round: Liberal 237, Moderate 236, Conservative 227; automatic runoff: Moderate 55-45). The IRV race on the same voters eliminates the Moderate in round one (fewest first choices) and elects the Liberal 51-49 — the classic center squeeze, in the article's own numbers. Companion election: 'electorate shifted left' (same candidates, the strong Liberal becomes the Condorcet winner).
